events.description: Iraqi forces invade Kuwait, triggering fears of supply disruption from two major OPEC producers and prompting a UN coalition response.
US-led coalition launches military action to expel Iraqi forces from Kuwait; Kuwaiti oil fields are set ablaze during the retreat.
Currency and financial crisis across East Asia sharply reduces regional energy demand expectations, contributing to a prolonged price slump into 1998-99.
OPEC and non-OPEC producers agree to significant output cuts, helping prices recover from 1998-99 lows.
Attacks on the US raise geopolitical uncertainty and briefly disrupt markets amid fears of broader Middle East conflict and a global demand slowdown.
US-led invasion of Iraq begins, raising concerns about the security of Iraqi and regional oil supply.
Brent/WTI prices reach record highs amid strong global demand, a weak US dollar, and speculative activity, shortly before the financial crisis.
Collapse of Lehman Brothers triggers the global financial crisis, causing a collapse in global oil demand expectations and a historic price crash.
Civil war disrupts Libyan crude exports, removing a significant volume of high-quality light crude from the market and pushing prices higher.
Rapid growth in US shale oil production creates a global supply glut, beginning a sustained decline in prices through late 2014.
OPEC (led by Saudi Arabia) opts to maintain output rather than cut, aiming to defend market share against US shale producers, accelerating the price collapse.
Implementation of the Iran nuclear deal lifts sanctions and allows Iranian oil back onto the market, coinciding with prices bottoming near $27/barrel.
OPEC and major non-OPEC producers (OPEC+) agree to coordinated production cuts for the first time since 2008, supporting a price recovery.

events.event_name: US withdraws from Iran nuclear deal
events.description: US exits the JCPOA and re-imposes sanctions on Iranian oil exports, tightening expected global supply.
Drone/missile strikes on Abqaiq and Khurais processing facilities knock out roughly half of Saudi oil output temporarily, causing the largest single-day price jump in years.
OPEC+ talks collapse; Saudi Arabia launches a price war by ramping up output and discounting crude, crashing prices amid falling COVID-19 demand.
Collapsing demand and storage capacity constraints during COVID-19 lockdowns briefly send US WTI futures negative; Brent also falls to multi-year lows.
OPEC+ agrees to a historic ~9.7 million barrel/day production cut to stabilize prices amid the pandemic demand shock.
Russia's invasion of Ukraine triggers Western sanctions on Russian energy exports and fears of major supply disruption, pushing Brent above $100/barrel.
